disease.name: G6PD Deficiency
disease.note: X-linked recessive deficiency of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase, the most common human enzyme defect (affecting 400 million people worldwide). Causes acute hemolytic anemia triggered by oxidative stress from drugs, infections, or fava beans. Neonatal jaundice is a common presentation. Highly prevalent in Africa, Mediterranean, Middle East, and Southeast Asia.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Acute hemolytic crisis with Hb <5 or renal failure -> EMERGENCY; Mild hemolysis -> OBSERVE; Neonate with jaundice -> URGENT phototherapy assessment

historyFeatures.note: Rapid onset of jaundice within 24-72h of trigger
May be from underlying infection triggering hemolysis
Sulfonamides, dapsone, primaquine, nitrofurantoin, aspirin, NSAIDs, quinolones, methylene blue
Favism: particularly Mediterranean variant; 24-48h after ingestion
Common trigger: bacterial pneumonia, hepatitis, typhoid

subtypes.name: G6PD A- Variant (African)
subtypes.management: Avoid triggers; supportive care during acute hemolysis; usually self-limiting because older cells are destroyed but younger cells with higher enzyme levels survive

subtypes.name: G6PD Mediterranean Variant
subtypes.management: Strict avoidance of ALL triggers; may require transfusion during acute hemolysis; neonatal screening important

subtypes.name: G6PD Deficiency with Neonatal Jaundice
subtypes.management: Phototherapy; exchange transfusion if bilirubin exceeds exchange threshold; identify G6PD deficiency in family; avoid naphthalene (mothballs) and henna

bedside.name: Urine dipstick
bedside.indication: positive for blood without RBCs on microscopy (hemoglobinuria); urobilinogen elevated

laboratory.name: FBC with reticulocyte count
laboratory.indication: acute drop in Hb; reticulocytosis develops 3-5 days after onset

laboratory.name: Blood film
laboratory.indication: bite cells (blister cells), hemighosts, polychromasia, 'ghost' RBCs; Heinz bodies on supravital stain

laboratory.name: G6PD enzyme assay (quantitative)
laboratory.indication: definitive diagnosis; LOW during hemolysis (older cells depleted); may be falsely normal during acute hemolysis (younger RBCs have higher enzyme) — repeat when recovered

laboratory.name: G6PD fluorescent spot test
laboratory.indication: rapid screening test; positive (no fluorescence) indicates deficiency

laboratory.name: Bilirubin (total and direct)
laboratory.indication: elevated unconjugated bilirubin; degree correlates with hemolysis severity

laboratory.name: Serum creatinine and electrolytes
laboratory.indication: monitor for acute kidney injury from hemoglobinuria

laboratory.name: Urine microscopy
laboratory.indication: hemoglobinuria (no RBCs despite dipstick positive); hemosiderin granules in urine (later)

laboratory.name: Direct Coombs test (DAT)
laboratory.indication: negative in G6PD deficiency (helps rule out AIHA)

laboratory.name: G6PD genetic testing (G6PD gene sequencing)
laboratory.indication: confirm variant type; useful for family counseling; not routinely needed

management.protocolReference: WHO Guidelines for G6PD Deficiency; UK Guidelines for G6PD Deficiency

pathways.criteria: Hb >7, no hemodynamic compromise, no renal failure, identifiable trigger removed
pathways.treatment: IMMEDIATELY stop all triggering drugs/agents. Identify and treat underlying infection if present. Hydration (IV fluids to maintain urine output >1mL/kg/h). Monitor Hb, bilirubin, LDH, renal function daily. Folic acid 5mg PO daily. Transfusion not needed if Hb >7 and stable. Most hemolysis is self-limited and resolves within 1-2 weeks. Patient education: avoid triggers lifelong. Provide G6PD wallet card.

pathways.criteria: Hb <7, rapid Hb drop (>2g/dL in 24h), hemodynamic compromise, or acute kidney injury
pathways.treatment: Hospital admission. IV fluids (normal saline or Ringer's lactate). Blood transfusion: cross-match compatible PRBC; transfuse carefully. Monitor for acute kidney injury (urine output, creatinine). Maintain urine output with IV fluids ± furosemide. Consider sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine (reduces hemoglobin precipitation in renal tubules). Renal replacement therapy if AKI refractory. Folic acid supplementation. Avoid ALL oxidative drugs.

pathways.criteria: Neonate (usually male) with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, family history or ethnic risk
pathways.treatment: Phototherapy per AAP/NICE bilirubin charts. Intensive phototherapy for moderate jaundice. Exchange transfusion if bilirubin exceeds exchange threshold. Identify and remove trigger (naphthalene mothballs, henna, certain drugs via breast milk). Check G6PD level (cord blood). Counsel mother about breastfeeding and avoiding triggers. Follow-up outpatient.

pathways.criteria: Known G6PD deficiency, asymptomatic, no acute hemolysis
pathways.treatment: Patient education: carry G6PD alert card or wear medical alert bracelet. AVOID: sulfonamides, dapsone, primaquine, nitrofurantoin, high-dose aspirin, methylene blue, phenazopyridine, quinolones (nalidixic acid, ciprofloxacin), chloramphenicol. Avoid fava beans (especially Mediterranean variant). Avoid naphthalene (mothballs). Treat infections promptly. Inform all healthcare providers. Screening of male children in family. Prenatal counseling for at-risk families.

disease.emergencyPriority: high if acute hemolysis; medium for neonatal jaundice
